summary: Steve Yegge introduces Gas Town, his fourth-generation AI agent orchestrator that coordinates 20-30 concurrent Claude Code instances to work on software projects simultaneously. Built on his earlier Beads issue tracker, Gas Town uses a Mad Max-themed hierarchy of worker roles (Mayor, Polecats, Refinery, Witness, Deacon, Dogs, Crew) managed through tmux. The system implements the MEOW (Molecular Expression of Work) stack, where workflows are expressed as chains of Beads issues that agents execute step-by-step, surviving crashes and context window limits. Yegge argues this represents the next evolution beyond single-agent coding, comparing Gas Town's architecture to Kubernetes and Temporal while noting it optimizes for work completion rather than uptime. He warns the system is expensive, chaotic, and only suitable for developers already comfortable juggling multiple AI coding agents.
key_points: Gas Town orchestrates 20-30 concurrent Claude Code instances through defined worker roles, using tmux as its primary UI
The MEOW stack (Beads, Epics, Molecules, Protomolecules, Formulas) provides durable workflow execution that survives agent crashes and context window limits
GUPP (Gastown Universal Propulsion Principle) keeps agents working by persisting work state in Git-backed Beads, so new sessions automatically resume interrupted work
The system achieves 'Nondeterministic Idempotence' — workflows complete reliably even though individual agent execution paths are unpredictable
Yegge presents an 8-stage evolution of AI-assisted development, positioning Gas Town as a tool only for developers at Stage 7+ who already manage 10+ concurrent agents
money_quotes: The industry is an embarrassing little kid's soccer team chasing the 2025 CLI form factor of Claude Code, rather than building what's next.
Gas Town is an industrialized coding factory manned by superintelligent robot chimps, and when they feel like it, they can wreck your shit in an instant.
Work becomes fluid, an uncountable substance that you sling around freely, like slopping shiny fish into wooden barrels at the docks.
Gas Town is me marching into the Church of Public Opinion on AI-Assisted Coding, lifting my leg, and ripping a fart that will be smelt all around the world.
You are a Product Manager, and Gas Town is an Idea Compiler.
Kubernetes asks 'Is it running?' while Gas Town asks 'Is it done?'
I'm tired of walking around and telling people the future, just waving it right in their faces, and not being believed.
key_insight: The next frontier in AI-assisted development isn't better individual coding agents but orchestration systems that coordinate dozens of agents into durable, self-healing workflows.
